title: Model Evaluation, Validation & Experimentation
pitchHtml: A model that looks good in a notebook and a model that moves the client's needle are two different things — and the gap between them is where most AI missions quietly fail. This module is about knowing whether a model is actually good, whether it's actually helping the business, and how to challenge an evaluation when a data scientist hands you one. For a tech lead, evaluation is not a maths topic; it's a risk-management and trust-building topic. The single most valuable thing you can learn here is to read an eval critically — because the number on the slide is almost never the number that matters.

items.html: Pick an evaluation metric from the business problem, not the textbook default — and defend the choice in one sentence
Read a confusion matrix, precision/recall trade-off, and ROC vs PR curve well enough to challenge them in a review
Insist on a baseline and explain why an accuracy number is meaningless without one
Spot data leakage and a contaminated test set before they reach production
Decide when an A/B test is needed, scope one, and name the alternative when you can't run it
Map a model metric to the client's KPI and call out when a team is optimising a proxy
Run a critical eval review that separates a real result from a flattering one

subsections.title: Pick the Metric from the Business Problem, Not the Textbook

blocks.html:
The first and most consequential decision in any evaluation is which number you optimise. Get this wrong and a technically excellent model solves the wrong problem. The metric must encode what the client actually cares about — the cost of each kind of mistake.

Classification — and accuracy's trap. Accuracy is "what fraction did we get right?" It is the default, and it is a trap on imbalanced data. If 1% of transactions are fraud, a model that predicts "never fraud" is 99% accurate and 100% useless. Whenever one class is rare or one mistake is costlier than the other, accuracy lies.

The honest vocabulary comes from the confusion matrix — four boxes, in plain language:

Model says positiveModel says negative
Actually positiveTrue Positive (correct catch)False Negative (missed it)
Actually negativeFalse Positive (false alarm)True Negative (correct pass)
Write those four counts as TP, FP, TN, FN and every classification metric is just a ratio of them:

Accuracy=TP+TNTP+TN+FP+FNPrecision=TPTP+FPRecall=TPTP+FNSpecificity=TNTN+FP\text{Accuracy}=\frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}\qquad \text{Precision}=\frac{TP}{TP+FP}\qquad \text{Recall}=\frac{TP}{TP+FN}\qquad \text{Specificity}=\frac{TN}{TN+FP}Accuracy=TP+TN+FP+FNTP+TN​Precision=TP+FPTP​Recall=TP+FNTP​Specificity=TN+FPTN​

In words: accuracy is the share of all calls that were correct; precision is "of the things we flagged, how many were real"; recall (sensitivity) is "of the things that were real, how many we caught"; specificity is recall's mirror for the negative class. Use: accuracy when classes are balanced and both errors cost the same; precision when a false alarm is expensive; recall when a miss is expensive; specificity when correctly clearing the negatives matters (e.g. not flagging healthy patients). From those four boxes come the metrics that matter:

MetricPlain-language questionReach for it when
PrecisionWhen we flag something, how often are we right?False alarms are expensive (e.g. blocking good customers)
Recall (sensitivity)Of all the real cases, how many did we catch?Misses are expensive (e.g. fraud, disease, churn)
F1A blend of precision and recall in one numberYou need a single figure and both errors hurt
ROC-AUCCan the model rank a random positive above a random negative?Classes are roughly balanced; you want a threshold-free score
PR-AUCSame ranking quality, but focused on the rare positive classImbalanced data — the one ROC-AUC flatters
F1 collapses precision (P) and recall (R) into a single score by taking their harmonic mean:
F1=2⋅P⋅RP+RF1 = 2\cdot\frac{P\cdot R}{P+R}F1=2⋅P+RP⋅R​
Use: when you need one headline number and both errors hurt.

blocks.title: why the harmonic mean, not the average
blocks.html: A plain average of P and R would let a model hide a disaster: precision 1.0 and recall 0.0 averages to a respectable 0.5. The harmonic mean refuses to be fooled — it is dragged toward the smaller of the two, so F1 only rises when both P and R are decent. It punishes imbalance between them, which is exactly what you want from a single "is this model any good at the rare class?" number. (F1 still ignores true negatives, so it's a poor fit when correctly clearing the negative class is what matters.)

blocks.title: precision vs recall is a dial, not a fact
blocks.html: A model outputs a score (a probability-like number); you choose a threshold above which you act. Slide the threshold down and you catch more real cases (recall up) but raise more false alarms (precision down). There is no universally "right" point — only the point that minimises your client's total cost. Threshold choice is a business decision, not a modelling one, and it should be made in a room with the people who live with the consequences.
ROC-AUC, without the integral. Where precision/recall judge the model at one threshold, ROC-AUC scores its ranking across all of them at once — and it has a clean plain-language meaning:

AUC=P(score(random positive)>score(random negative))\text{AUC} = P\big(\text{score}(\text{random positive}) > \text{score}(\text{random negative})\big)AUC=P(score(random positive)>score(random negative))
That is: pick one real positive and one real negative at random; AUC is the probability the model gives the positive the higher score. 0.5 is a coin flip (no skill), 1.0 is a perfect ranker.

blocks.title: why AUC is threshold-independent (and when it lies)
blocks.html: AUC depends only on the order the model puts cases in, never on where you draw the line, so it answers "can this model tell positives from negatives at all?" before any business threshold is chosen — handy for comparing models early. Its blind spot is imbalance: with 1% positives, the huge pile of true negatives makes the false-positive rate barely budge, so AUC can look excellent while the model is useless on the rare class. That's why on skewed data you switch to PR-AUC, which only looks at precision and recall and ignores the easy true negatives — it tells the uncomfortable truth ROC-AUC flatters away.

Cost-sensitive evaluation makes this explicit. Attach a euro figure to a false positive and a false negative, and the "best" model is the one with the lowest expected cost — which is frequently not the most accurate one. A fraud model that catches 90% of fraud while occasionally annoying a good customer can beat one that catches 70% with zero annoyance, if a missed fraud costs far more than a false alarm. The matrix only becomes a decision once money is on it.

Regression — what each error punishes. When you're predicting a number (demand, price, time), the metric choice is about how you feel about big misses:

MetricWhat it measuresPunishesUse when
MAEAverage size of the error, in real unitsAll errors equallyErrors scale linearly with cost; you want an interpretable "off by X on average"
RMSELike MAE but squares errors firstLarge errors disproportionatelyOne big miss is much worse than several small ones
MAPEAverage error as a percentageRelative errors; explodes near zeroStakeholders think in % and values are comfortably above zero
For n predictions y^i\hat{y}_iy^​i​ against actuals yiy_iyi​:

RMSE=1n∑i=1n(yi−y^i)2MAE=1n∑i=1n∣yi−y^i∣MAPE=100%n∑i=1n∣yi−y^iyi∣\text{RMSE}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i-\hat{y}_i)^2}\qquad \text{MAE}=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}|y_i-\hat{y}_i|\qquad \text{MAPE}=\frac{100\%}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left|\frac{y_i-\hat{y}_i}{y_i}\right|RMSE=n1​i=1∑n​(yi​−y^​i​)2​MAE=n1​i=1∑n​∣yi​−y^​i​∣MAPE=n100%​i=1∑n​​yi​yi​−y^​i​​​

In words: MAE is the average miss in the unit you predicted ("off by 12 units on average"); RMSE is similar but squares each error before averaging; MAPE averages the error as a percentage of the actual value.

blocks.title: why RMSE punishes big misses harder than MAE
blocks.html: Because RMSE squares every error before averaging, a single error of 10 contributes 100 while ten errors of 1 contribute only 10 in total — so one large miss dominates the score. MAE, summing raw absolute errors, treats that same big miss as merely ten small ones added up. Practical consequence: optimise RMSE when one catastrophic error (a stock-out, a wildly wrong price) is far worse than many small ones; optimise MAE when every unit of error costs the same. MAPE re-expresses error in percentage terms — intuitive for stakeholders, but it blows up as actuals approach zero and over-penalises under-forecasts, so keep it away from low-volume or near-zero series.

A demand forecast judged on RMSE will work hard to avoid the occasional catastrophic stock-out; the same forecast judged on MAPE will care more about percentage accuracy on small-volume items. Same model, different "best" — because the metric encodes a value judgement. (Forecasting metrics in practice: Classical ML & Forecasting in Practice.)

Ranking & recommendation, in a sentence. When the output is an ordered list (search results, recommended products), accuracy is the wrong lens entirely — what matters is whether the good items are near the top. The family to recognise is Precision@K / Recall@K (did we get the top-K right?) and NDCG (are the best items ranked highest?). You don't need the formulas; you need to ask "ranked how, and judged at what cut-off?"

blocks.title: Note
blocks.html: Evaluation of LLM and generative outputs (no single ground truth, LLM-as-judge, hallucination/faithfulness scoring) is a different discipline and lives in Large Language Models in Practice. Don't reach for accuracy/F1 there.

A metric is a number with no meaning until you compare it to something. A model is "good" only relative to the alternative the client would otherwise use. "Our churn model is 82% accurate" is not a result — it's a number waiting for context.

Three baselines you should demand before accepting any headline metric:

BaselineWhat it isWhat it tells you
Naïve / trivialAlways predict the majority class, the mean, or "same as last week"The floor — beat this or the model is noise
Current processWhat the business does today (a rule, a human, a spreadsheet)The only bar that matters commercially
Simplest modelA logistic regression / a single ruleWhether the complex model earns its complexity

blocks.title: the 82% that meant nothing
blocks.html: A team proudly reports an 82%-accurate churn classifier. You ask one question: what's the baseline rate? It turns out only 18% of customers churn — so "predict nobody churns" is 82% accurate too. The model has, so far, demonstrated zero skill. Reframed on the metric that matters (recall on churners, at a threshold tuned to the retention team's call capacity), the model catches 46% of churners versus the marketing team's current 31% from gut-feel targeting. That is the result: +15 points of recall against the real-world process — translatable into retained revenue. The 82% was a vanity number; the comparison to the current process was the business case.
The discipline: never present or accept a model metric in isolation. Always pair it with "...versus what?"

Evaluation only means something if the model is judged on data it has never seen. The mechanics are simple to state and easy to get subtly wrong.

Train / validation / test — three jobs, not two.

  • Training set — the model learns its parameters here.
  • Validation set — you tune choices here (which model, which threshold, which knobs). Used many times.
  • Test set — judged once, at the end, to estimate real-world performance. It is sacred: the moment you make a decision based on the test set, it stops being an unbiased estimate and silently becomes another validation set. Treat it like a sealed exam.

Cross-validation, intuitively. When data is scarce, holding out a single validation slice wastes data and gives a noisy estimate. Cross-validation rotates the held-out slice: split into k folds, train on k−1, validate on the remaining one, repeat k times, average. You get a more stable performance estimate and a sense of its variance — a model whose score swings wildly across folds is fragile, and that variance is itself a finding worth reporting.

blocks.title: leakage is "studying the exam in advance"
blocks.html: Data leakage is when information from the future, or from the answer, sneaks into training — so the model looks brilliant offline and collapses in production. The classic traps: scaling or imputing using statistics computed over the whole dataset before splitting; including a feature that's only known after the outcome (a "paid_late_fee" flag predicting "will default"); or — the cross-validation-specific killer — fitting any preprocessing step on all the data and then cross-validating, which leaks the validation folds into training every single round. The fix is a rule: fit every transformation inside the training fold only, then apply it outward. (Leakage is born upstream, in feature engineering — see Working with Data: Sources, Quality & Feature Engineering.)

For a tech lead, the practical check is one question to the data scientist: "Walk me through your split — when was each transformation fit, and could any feature contain information you wouldn't have at prediction time?" If the answer is vague, the eval is suspect.

This is the trap that humbles experienced teams. Great offline metrics do not guarantee business impact. A model can ace the held-out test set and still fail in production, because the offline world differs from the live one in ways the test set can't capture:

  • Distribution shift — live traffic doesn't look like your historical data (new users, seasonality, a marketing campaign).
  • Feedback loops — the model's own actions change the world it then sees (recommend a product → it sells more → the data now says it's popular).
  • The metric isn't the outcome — a more "accurate" recommender can reduce revenue if its accuracy comes from recommending things people would have bought anyway.
  • Integration reality — latency, stale features, a UI that buries the output, users who ignore it.

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: Offline evaluation answers "is the model technically capable?" Online evaluation answers "does deploying it actually change behaviour and outcomes?" They are different questions, and only the second pays the bills. Offline is a necessary filter (don't ship a model that fails offline), never a sufficient proof. The bridge between them is a controlled experiment on live traffic — which is the next concept.

To know whether a model causes a better outcome, you compare it against the status quo on live traffic, at the same time, on comparable users. That's an A/B test: randomly split users into a control group (current experience) and a treatment group (the model), run both in parallel, and measure the difference in the metric that matters.

The leader-relevant pieces, intuition only:

  • Hypothesis first. State what you expect to move, by how much, before you start ("the new model lifts conversion by ≥1.5 points"). No hypothesis, no experiment — just data-dredging.
  • Statistical power & sample size. To detect a small effect you need many users; to detect a large one, fewer. Power is the test's ability to notice a real effect when it exists. The practical consequence: smaller expected lifts need bigger samples and longer runs, and you should estimate roughly how long before launching — a low-traffic product may simply be unable to detect a 0.5% lift in any reasonable time, which itself is a decision-relevant finding.

PitfallWhat goes wrongThe discipline
PeekingStopping the moment the result looks significantFix the duration/sample up front; don't stop early on a whim
Multiple comparisonsTest 20 metrics, one looks "significant" by chancePre-declare the primary metric; treat the rest as exploratory
Novelty effectUsers react to newness, not real value; the lift fadesRun long enough to see the effect settle
Contaminated groupsTreatment leaks into control (shared accounts, network effects)Randomise at the right unit; check the split is clean

When you can't run an A/B test. Sometimes randomisation is impossible — too little traffic, ethical or contractual constraints, a change that hits everyone at once (a pricing model, a back-office tool). The honest answer is not "skip evaluation"; it's to reach for a weaker-but-real causal design:

ApproachWhenCaveat
Before / afterCheap, no split possibleConfounded by anything else that changed (seasonality, the economy) — weakest evidence
Holdout groupYou can withhold the change from a slice indefinitelyA standing control; clean but "wastes" the holdout's potential gains
Geo / regional testRoll out by region, compare to held-back regionsRegions must be comparable; spillover between them muddies it

These are the intuition; the proper machinery for "did X cause Y when I couldn't randomise" is causal inference — gestured at here, treated in Causal Inference & Experimentation (and deeper still in the AI Scientist path).

The single most common disconnect in AI delivery: the model's metric is not the client's KPI. AUC, F1, RMSE — these are instruments. The client cares about retained revenue, hours saved, cost avoided, NPS. Your job as tech lead is to build and defend the bridge between the two, and to stay alert to its weak joints.

blocks.title: when optimising the proxy backfires
blocks.html: An e-commerce team optimises a recommender for click-through rate (the model metric) as a proxy for revenue (the business KPI). CTR climbs 20% — a triumph on the dashboard. But revenue is flat, because the model learned to surface cheap, clicky, low-margin items. The proxy improved while the goal stagnated. The lesson isn't "CTR is bad" — it's that a proxy is only as good as its link to the real outcome, and that link must be checked, not assumed. When you can't measure the real KPI directly in the experiment, choose the proxy most tightly coupled to it and treat the coupling itself as a thing to validate.

The mapping is a deliverable in its own right: model metric → leading indicator → business KPI → € impact, with each arrow's assumption written down. A team that can't draw this chain doesn't yet know what "good" means.

subsections.title: How to Read a Data Scientist's Eval Critically

blocks.html: This is the capstone skill of the module — the questions that, asked in a review, separate a robust result from a flattering one. You don't need to out-maths the data scientist; you need to ask the right things and listen for confident, specific answers.

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: A weak evaluation almost always fails on one of three fronts: no honest comparison (no baseline, wrong metric), a contaminated estimate (leakage, a peeked-at test set, the wrong split), or a missing bridge to the business (offline only, optimising a proxy). The questions below probe exactly those three seams.

• "What's the baseline, and how much better is this than the current process?" — exposes vanity numbers.
  • "Why this metric? What does a false positive cost the client versus a false negative?" — exposes a textbook-default choice.
  • "Show me the confusion matrix / the errors, not just the headline score." — exposes a number hiding a lopsided failure mode.
  • "How was the data split, and when was each transformation fit?" — exposes leakage.
  • "Has the test set been used to make any decisions?" — exposes a burned test set.
  • "How will this offline metric translate to a business outcome, and how will we confirm it live?" — exposes the offline≠online gap.
  • "What's the variance across folds / how stable is this result?" — exposes fragility dressed up as a single confident number.

If the answers are vague or defensive, the problem is the evaluation, not the question.

Evaluation and experimentation sell as trust and rigour — the work that lets a client believe (and a board sign off on) an AI investment. These missions punch above their size because they de-risk far larger ones.

  • Model Audit / Independent Validation ("second opinion") — Problem: a client has a model (theirs or a vendor's) about to go live, or already live, and someone senior wants an independent check before betting on it. Deliverable: an audit report — metric appropriateness, baseline comparison, leakage check, a re-run on held-out or fresh data, and a go / fix / no-go recommendation with the reasoning. Scope: 1-3 weeks, fixed price. Who buys: a CTO/CDO de-risking a launch, a risk/compliance function, an investor doing technical due diligence. This is the highest-trust, highest-margin mission in this module — you're selling judgement, not build.
  • Experimentation / A-B Testing Capability Setup — Problem: the client ships changes (and models) on opinion because they have no way to measure causal impact. Deliverable: an experimentation framework — assignment/randomisation logic, a metrics layer with primary and guardrail metrics, a sample-size/duration calculator, and a lightweight experiment-review ritual. Scope: 4-8 weeks. Who buys: product/growth leadership, a data platform owner. (Lands on the data platform — see Data Engineering & Platforms — and feeds MLOps & Productionization.)
  • Define Success Metrics & Evaluation Plan for an AI Initiative — Problem: a project is starting (or floundering) with no agreed definition of "good." Deliverable: a one-page evaluation plan — the chosen model metric, its mapping to the business KPI, the baseline, the validation protocol, and the offline→online plan. Scope: 1-2 weeks, often the opening act of a larger build. Who buys: the project's executive sponsor; an internal team wanting an external sign-off on their plan.

Buy-vs-build for the client: the framework (an experimentation platform) can be bought or assembled from existing tools — sell the design, the metric definitions, and the rigour, which are bespoke. What to charge for: the audit and the evaluation plan as fixed-price, repeatable IP; the capability setup as a scoped build. What NOT to promise: that an audit will bless a model (it may condemn it — that's the value); a specific lift from an A/B test before it's run; or that an experiment will be conclusive when the client's traffic is too thin to power it — surface that constraint up front, don't discover it after eight weeks.
As of 2026 — treat names/prices as perishable; re-check before quoting.

Tool / CategoryUse hereOSS / PaidNote
scikit-learn metricsStandard offline metrics & confusion matricesOSSThe default; covers most classification/regression needs
Experiment-tracking (MLflow, Weights & Biases)Logging runs, metrics, comparing modelsOSS / FreemiumKeeps evals reproducible; deeper use → MLOps & Productionization
A/B platforms (Optimizely, GrowthBook, Statsig, LaunchDarkly)Running & analysing online experimentsPaid / OSS (GrowthBook)Buy the assignment + stats engine; don't hand-roll significance
Statistics libraries (statsmodels, scipy.stats)Sample-size & significance under the hoodOSSPowers a homegrown framework when a platform is overkill
Model-validation / monitoring (Evidently, Fiddler, Arize)Drift detection, offline→online gap, live monitoringOSS / PaidThe bridge from "good offline" to "still good in production"
Notebooks + a clear templateThe actual audit deliverableOSSFor a model audit, a rigorous reproducible notebook is the product
For most audits and evaluation plans, the heavy tooling is optional — the deliverable is a disciplined analysis and a defensible recommendation, not a platform.

SituationHeavy evaluation/experimentation is overkill — do this instead
A reversible, low-stakes change (button colour, copy tweak)Ship it, watch the metric, roll back if it dips — a full A/B test isn't worth the setup
Traffic is far too low to ever reach significanceUse judgement + a before/after sanity check; be honest that it won't be conclusive
A one-off internal analysis, not a recurring decisionA clean train/test split and a baseline is enough; skip the experimentation platform
The model already obviously beats a trivial baseline by miles, low riskA single held-out test + a baseline suffices; don't gold-plate the validation
No real outcome to measure yet (pure exploration)Define the success metric first (Mission 3 above) — measuring comes after
The mirror image of "you don't need AI for this" is "you don't need a full experiment for this." Matching the rigour to the stakes is itself the senior judgement.

• Chip Huyen — Designing Machine Learning Systems — the offline/online gap, evaluation in production, and monitoring, framed pragmatically. (Paid)
  • Kohavi, Tang & Xu — Trustworthy Online Controlled Experiments — the definitive practical guide to A/B testing and its many traps. (Paid)

• scikit-learn's Model evaluation user-guide section — a clear, example-led tour of the metrics. Free.
  • Google's Rules of Machine Learning (Martin Zinkevich) — battle-tested guidance on metrics, baselines, and not fooling yourself. Free.

• Deeper math (estimators, statistical learning theory, the maths of evaluation & experimentation) → Model Selection, Evaluation & AutoML and Causal Inference & Experimentation (AI Scientist Path).
  • Deeper code (implementing metrics, cross-validation pipelines, experiment tracking & a model registry) → Classical ML Mastery and Experiment Tracking & Model Registry (ML Engineer Path).

items.html: Is the metric tied to the business cost of each error — not a textbook default?
Is there a baseline (naïve, current process, simplest model) the result is compared against?
Was the test set held out and judged once, with no decisions made on it?
Was every transformation fit inside the training data only (no leakage)?
Is there a stated plan to confirm the offline result on live traffic?
Is the model metric mapped to a business KPI, with the link checked, not assumed?
Do we know how stable the result is (variance across folds / confidence)?

blocks.html:
• A headline accuracy with no baseline and no class balance mentioned.
  • "We picked F1 because it's standard." (Standard for what cost structure?)
  • The test set was used to choose the model or the threshold. (It's no longer a test set.)
  • Offline metrics presented as proof of business impact.
  • An A/B test stopped early because it "looked significant."
  • Twenty metrics measured, the one winner highlighted.
  • The model metric improved but nobody can state the KPI it should move.
  • "We'll validate it once it's live" — with no actual experiment design.

subsections.title: Scoping Prompts (ask the client / the team)

blocks.html:
• "How do you define success today, in numbers the business recognises?"
  • "What does a false positive cost you, versus a false negative?"
  • "What do you do now, without this model, and how well does it work?"
  • "How would we prove, on live traffic, that this actually changed outcomes?"
  • "Can you randomise users — and if not, what's the cleanest comparison we can make?"
  • "Has anyone independently checked this model, or only the team that built it?"
Before moving on, you should be able to:

items.html: Choose and justify an evaluation metric from a business problem, including the threshold trade-off
Explain why a given accuracy number is or isn't meaningful by naming the right baseline
Detect leakage and a burned test set from how the validation was set up
Explain why an offline win can fail online, and design the experiment that would confirm it
Scope an A/B test — or name and justify the alternative when randomisation is impossible
Run a critical eval review whose questions expose a weak evaluation
